sentences.text: Yes.
Finish.
Whatever I'm saying.
Yes, OK.
The first name is Livia.
L-I-V-I-A.
The last name is hyphenated, Bitton-Jackson.
B-I-T-T-O-N hyphen, Jackson.
Friedmann, F-R-I-E-D-M-A-N-N.
Wow, that's a tough one.
I was born on February 28, 1931, so I'm 64 plus.
I was born in a small town in Czechoslovakia.
And the town has actually two variations of the name.
In-- during the Czechoslovak era, it was called Samorin, which is S-A-M-O-R-I-N.
And then later when the Hungarians occupied it, it is Somorja.
S-O-M-O-R-J-A.
Well, the town was a very small town and very quiet.
We didn't have any cars.
We had only horse-drawn wagons.
One main street, that was really the only actual street.
And that was the only paved street.
The school where I went to was at the other end of the town.
We lived in a section which was far from the Jewish neighborhood.
The Jewish neighborhood had the synagogue, the school.
And that was all in one big yard and all the other institutions that related to whatever Jewish needs, the assembly meeting hall, et cetera, et cetera.
And where we lived was not the Jewish neighborhood, so all my friends were gentile girls, mostly Roman Catholic, some Lutheran.
There were Lutherans and Roman Catholics in our town.
And only when I started to-- let's say, the junior high, so to speak-- did I really have Jewish girlfriends where I would then, on my own, go to the other section of town where the Jews lived.
And a very quiet town.
Now, from the point of view of being under Czechoslovak rule is that, of course, I was a very small girl then.
But Czechoslovakia was the only democracy in Europe.
It was a very advanced economy.
It was a very, very, lovely open society.
So, I personally don't have specific recollections.
But for me, it was a very, very, happy memory, my childhood, before the Hungarian occupation.
My father, my mother, and I have an older brother.
We also had an aunt, my mother's oldest sister, who lived in town, some 8 to 10 minute walk from our house.
And she practically was part of our family.
She was there at festive meals.
At the Sabbath Eves, she ate with us.
She was practically part of our family.

And then, both my mother and my father's family, brothers and sisters, lived in Hungary, which was even though it was quite near the other side of the Danube, but the two countries were not friendly to each other.
And so the travel wasn't that easy.
People had to apply for a passport and a visa.
And a visa was not always easy to get, so that the visits between my extended family and us weren't frequent.
Possibly once a year, my mother's brothers and one sister would come to our town.
Because my grandparents were buried there.
Meaning, their parents were buried.
And it is a Jewish custom to go at least once a year to pay respect to the departed.
So this I remember as a child, that all my uncles would come and my aunt from Hungary at least once a year.
My father's brother, my father had one younger brother, he went to America, to New York, when I was a very, very little girl.
So I never met him until after the war when I got here.
And then he had a sister, who had a very large family.
And she had never come to us.
I visited them only once in 1939 after the Hungarian occupation when it was easy to just travel.
Because we, too, belonged to Hungary.
So I met them only then, and it was a very happy visit.
It was a large family with six children.
And they were all taken to Auschwitz and did not return.
My grandmother was still alive, my mother's mother.
Otherwise, I did not know my grandparents.
My mother's parents had died before I was born.
I only knew them, that my mother would visit the cemetery.
And I would go with her every time she visited their graves.
Well, my father had a general store.
It was a grocery, and it included many other items like liquors, and some dry goods, and some animal feed.
It was just like a general store in a small, small town.
And it occupied-- our house occupied a whole corner, sort of three sides to the street.
And the store was-- storefront was on the main street.
And the entrance to our residential area was the other side of the street.
But it was a well-known store.
It had a huge sign.
And the people called that square the Friedmann Corner.
So we lived on the Friedmann corner.
My father had a huge sign that said Markus Friedmann.
That was his name.
And so it was a happy time, because all the customers who would come to the store didn't just come in and shop and leave.
But people would come in and stay and talk.
And my mother loved company.
And so we would have many chairs around the store.
And then the customers would just sit there and talk, and then do their shopping and leave, and others would come in.
And it was like a very social place, our store.
I have very, very happy memories of that.
Many of the farmers-- it was a farming community-- would come in and the farmers' wives.
And I would know everybody intimately and their children.
They would sit sometimes for hours, sometimes not even on chairs, but on sacks of animal feed.
We had all kinds of sacks with barley and wheat.
And, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] oh, I can't remember all the names of the various grains that were in large sacks.
And as the sack was getting lower and lower, it was very convenient to sit on and people would sit and-- and just chat.
So, and these were all not Jewish people.
These were all gentiles.
And I never knew the difference.
As a matter of fact, I didn't even know I was Jewish.
I-- I just grew up.
We were Orthodox Jews.
And we're observing all the Jewish laws and customs.
But that didn't tell me that I was Jewish.
I didn't know to the extent that I have a recollection that my mother told me later, that she overheard me while she was in the store.
And I was right in front talking to one of my friends.
And I must have been about five.
And her-- my friend said, you know, the Jew came today to us to buy a goose.
Now, the local Jews were not called the Jew by these children.
This was a Jew from probably another town who came to look for geese or chickens or for other farm goods.
And he wore a beard.
You see, all the Jews in our town did not have beards.
It was a strictly Orthodox community, but all the men were clean shaven.
So they were not called Jew.
Only a Jew with a beard was a Jew.
And-- because I see [?
Jason ?] has a beard.
But that's how it was in Europe.
Only Jews, very pious Jews, would wear beards.
So my friend, [INAUDIBLE] her name was, said, you know, the Jew came to our house to buy a goose.
And I said, did he leave all ready?
She says, yes, he left.
Oh, what a pity, I said.
I would love to have seen him, because I've never seen a Jew.
And my mother is listening to this.
And she was laughing.
And [INAUDIBLE] promised me that next time the Jew comes, she'll call me, so I can see a Jew.
And later, she called me over.
And she said, you never saw a Jew?
And I said, no.
And she said to me, well, you're a Jew.
And I said, what are you talking about?
And I was very surprised.
And only then did they explain to me that being a Jew doesn't mean you have a beard.
Some Jews have beards, some not.
It's a religion.
And our religion is Jewish.
And [INAUDIBLE] is Lutheran.
And [INAUDIBLE], my other friend, is Catholic.
These are various religions.
And so we are Jews.
And that's when I discovered that I was a Jew.
So it was-- we were completely oblivious to the fact.
Exactly 100.
And I found that out when the persecution came in.
We started-- we had to be registered and taken to the ghetto.
And only then, I found out 100 families. 505 souls to be exact.
I started school at age six.
At five, I was going to kindergarten, which was not part of the school.
This was also run by a gentile woman not far from where we lived.
And so I went.
It was rather a private kindergarten.
But then, at age six, I started to go to school.
And this is really when I first met the Jewish children at the other end of school.
But I didn't really have personal friends among them, because they all lived far.
And my mother didn't let me walk alone that-- that far.
Only by the age of 9 or 10 did I start to have close Jewish friends as well.
Yes, yes.
The area where I lived is a strip of land alongside the Danube.
And not only, actually, the entire eastern segment of Czechoslovakia which is, by the way, today Slovakia.
And that entire area was annexed to Hungary as a deal between Hitler and Horthy, the Hungarian Regent.
And it was-- the Hungarian Regent was an ally of Hitler.
So-- and Czechoslovakia was not.
And so the Western segment was occupied by Germany.
And the Eastern segment was annexed to Hungary.
And when the Hungarians marched in, at first we did not know that there will be a change.
We did not realize that the Hungarians were going to be terribly anti-Semitic and hostile to us.
My mother, who-- whose mother tongue was Hungarian and never really learned Slovak, was very happy at first when the Hungarians were coming.
Such a misconception, she thought, oh, how wonderful.
Now, I'm going to understand even what the soldiers are singing as they're marching through the town.
Because our town was a military headquarters.
It was near Bratislava, which was the capital of Slovakia, and also near the Danube.
So it was a military headquarters.
And very often the soldiers of the Czechoslovak army would march through the town whenever they were going somewhere for maneuvers.
And they were singing Slovak.
And some of our customers were speaking Slovak.
The majority spoke Hungarian.
But whenever my mother had to speak Slovak to customers, she would have difficulty.
And she would call my father to the store who came from an area of Slovakia where Slovak was primarily spoken.
So his language was [? rather ?] Slovak.
And he knew Hungarian much less, while my mother was the other way around.
So when the soldiers would march she never understood what they are singing.
And she would always say, ah, I feel so-- so alien.
I don't understand Slovak.
So when the Hungarians marched in, she said, oh, how wonderful.
Now, everybody will speak Hungarian.
It's so good for me.
Well, very soon, the Hungarian soldiers as they were marching, they were singing anti-Semitic ditties.
And one of them was, I remember in Hungarian-- [HUNGARIAN] Berger is a Hungarian name.
And they sort of played on that name.
A Jewish name, I mean-- Berger.
So they said, oh, Berger is a-- is a crook.
And every Jew should be hanged on the gallows.
This to a rhyme, this they were singing.
Or, about Jewish girls, you know, all kinds of insinuating songs.
So when they were singing these anti-Semitic songs as they were marching through, very early, shortly after the occupation, so my father said, aren't you glad you can understand what they're singing?
You know, sarcastically.
So, and then very soon other changes took place.
The Hungarians, very quickly, closed our store.
Not all Jewish stores were closed.
Ours was the first in town, because it was in the Jewish-- in the gentile neighborhood.
And they said, well, we want to give this business to the gentile store owners.
And very quickly they used the term Aryan.
The Aryan store owners should make money.
And so they closed our store very quickly.
And there were other restrictions.
A Jew law was passed which was stricter than the Jew law passed in Germany.
You know, in 1935, the Nuremberg law was passed which stripped the citizenship from the Jews of Germany and other restrictions.
The Hungarian Jew law was-- went much further.
It-- in 1938, it was passed, very shortly after occupation.
We were not permitted, for instance, to slaughter beef.
We could eat only chickens and geese.
They took away-- very quickly, everybody else's store was afterwards closed.
We were not permitted to read newspapers.
They took away our radios.
We shouldn't be able to hear the news from abroad.
Because they said that the Hungarians-- that the Jews are enemy aliens.
That's how we were classified, and to make sure that you should not be able to listen to the news from England or the United States.
The allies were very, very-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] considered, you know, no, no.
And so we shouldn't be able to contact them.
And, of course, shortly afterwards, the war broke out in 1939.
And we had to-- there was a blackout every night.
And the-- the police would come especially to the Jewish homes to check if they are very carefully closing their shades.
Because they always said that the Jews probably will try to signal the allied planes with lights and all kinds of paranoid things they believed about the Jews.
And also, they conducted what they called raids on our homes.
That quite unexpectedly, mostly in the middle of the night, the Hungarian military police would come.
They would knock on the doors, and we had to open.
And they would march in, two or three of them.
And they would search the house.
They would have a warrant to search the house.
The search meant that they would open all the closets, spill out all the contents, stand up on top of the furniture, look behind curtains, poke even our couch and beds with their bayonets.
And what were they searching for?
They were searching for weapons.
You can imagine that a small town person never even dreamt of seeing a weapon other than in the hands of the soldiers or the military police.
Weapons for-- concealed radio equipment, that we shouldn't contact secretly the allies about where the war is standing, et cetera.
But in the process of doing this, they would take things, whatever they saw.
If a-- if a policeman would see a silver object, like there's a silver cup over there in the cabinet, then quickly he would put it in his pocket.
He would see a watch.
In his other pocket, a bottle of perfume.
Whatever quickly they would notice quickly, they would put in their pockets.
And they didn't even do it surreptitiously.
So we could see it, and there was nothing we could do.
We just watched them violate our privacy and take our possessions.
And we had beautiful embroidered chairs, Goblin chairs, in the-- in the dining room.
And sometimes in the winter, they would come with muddy boots.
And they would not pull over a wooden chair to stand up if they want to reach a higher closet.
But they would always stand on those chairs and mess them up.
And, you know, quite early on, you felt terribly humiliated and helpless and-- and completely exposed to violence of this sort.
So this was the beginning.
And for us, it was pretty, pretty bad.
You know, now, looking back in retrospect what we've gone through later, it seems small item.
But then it was very frightening and very degrading.
And to me, it was very scary when I see them do this.
And also, the appearance of the Hungarian military police was very scary.
And they had these high helmets, metal high helmets with cock feathers in it, high, the feathers.
They called them the cock feathers.
That was the military police.
And they would come with weapons slung on their shoulder, with the bayonet sticking up.
And that's how they would march.
They all had mustaches.
And it was very scary.
So this was-- this was more or less the beginning, the quite early years, '39, '40, '41.
As we progress, it was getting worse and worse.
There were all kinds of other things.
Like, by 1942, they would sometimes find something that they would say was a violation.
Let's say, they would find wool.
My mother was knitting a sweater.
And they said, where did you get this wool?
I don't know, I bought it in the store.
He says, well, this is English wool.
This comes from England.
You imported this from England.
So my mother said, we've never imported anything, ever.
We buy everything local.
He says, no, this is English wool.
You imported it.
So they would arrest my father for that.
And they would take him away, and we wouldn't know where he was.
And sometimes for days he was interrogated by the Hungarian military police, which was very much like the Gestapo.
Sometimes he was beaten.
It was well known that they would tell the men they would put their feet into a bowl of hot water.
He would have to keep his feet in there until the soles of his foot would become very tender.
And then they would take whips and start whipping him.
And the soles of his foot they would whip.
And they would say, reveal spies.
There's spies among you.
Who is a spy in town?
And my father would say, there are no spies here.
You know, you must visualize our town.
Everyone was a small town businessman.
The Jews were not farmers.
Because that was an ancient thing that Jews were not permitted to own land.
Even then, by the time Czechoslovakia was a-- a democracy.
And Jews were permitted to own land.
By that time, hundreds of years of culture brought about that the Jews were all business people.
They were not farmers.
But other than that, they were very small town business people.
They-- people had groceries.
And they had dry goods stores.
They would have textile shops, small town people who mingled with the farmers.
As a matter of fact, I would go with my friends to help out in the summer with harvesting.
You know, in our country, then harvesting was done by hand.
No machines.
They would be a harvester there who went ahead with the huge sickle and would cut the grain.
And then the women would walk behind him and would have to pick up a bundle, and then a man would-- and put a bundle on the side, and again another bundle, in a beautiful row.
And then another farmer would go and tie them into sheaves.
And so children would help out, do this one line.
So we were very much part of the scenery.
And this was very bucolic, very country, very a good culture.
And this was the Jews as well.
There was no difference.
But now, they wanted to know spies.
And my father kept saying, there are no spies.
And I cannot tell you.
And sometimes they would just keep on beating him on the had satisfied their whatever hatred they had against Jews.
And then they would let him go.
And then for days, he couldn't walk on his feet.
Because the feet would get blistering and painful.
This was going on until the Germans occupied Hungary.
So that gradually, gradually we were eased into the kind of horror that-- what's to await us later on.
Yes.
That is a good question.
Well, first of all--
Yes.
